sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Cherry Growers Australia, Australian state agriculture departments (DPIRD and Business Queensland), horticultural research and recognised industry sources.

core.intro: Cherries are the showpiece of the summer fruit bowl, but they are also the most demanding of the backyard stone fruits: they want a genuinely cold winter, a pollination partner for most varieties, and netting against birds. Get the variety and the site right and a tree will reward you for decades. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: The cultivated cherries are the sweet cherry (Prunus avium), grown for fresh eating, and the sour or acid cherry (Prunus cerasus, such as Morello), grown mainly for cooking. Most named trees are sweet cherries: dark types like Bing, Lapins and Stella, and pale, yellow-blushed types like Napoleon and the Rainier types. Sour cherries are smaller, hardier, more shade-tolerant trees whose sharp fruit is for pies, jam and bottling, and they are self-fertile. For a small garden, genetic-dwarf cherries (sold under names like Trixzie) stay around two metres and crop in a pot. Decide on use and flavour first, then match the variety to your winter chill and pollination needs.

sections.heading: Winter chill: the make or break factor
sections.body: Cherries are deciduous and need a long spell of winter cold to wake up and crop, and they are the most chill-demanding of the common stone fruits: most sweet cherries want roughly 800 to 1200 hours below about 7 degrees. Plant a high-chill variety where winters are too mild and you get weak, straggly flowering and almost no fruit, which is why cherries fail across most of warm Australia. The exception is the low-chill Royal series bred in California (Minnie Royal, Royal Lee and Royal Crimson), which need only about 250 hours and have made cherries possible in milder districts, though they still want a cool site. Matching the variety's chill to your district is the single most important choice you make.

sections.heading: Pollination: do you need two trees?
sections.body: Most sweet cherries cannot set fruit with their own pollen and need a second, compatible variety flowering at the same time, with bees to carry the pollen: caged trees with no pollinator set almost nothing, while well-pollinated trees set heavily. Several modern varieties are self-fertile and will crop alone, including Stella (the first self-fertile sweet cherry), Lapins, Sunburst, Starkrimson and Sir Don. Many classic varieties, including Bing, Napoleon and the Rainier types, need a pollinator, and some old favourites cannot pollinate each other because they share the same incompatibility group. The low-chill Minnie Royal and Royal Lee must be grown together, because each pollinates the other (Royal Crimson, which is self-fertile, partners both). If you have room for only one tree, choose a self-fertile variety or a multi-graft tree that carries a compatible pair.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give cherries full sun and a deep, free-draining loam at a slightly acid pH of about 6.0 to 6.5. They hate wet feet more than most fruit trees, and a few days of waterlogging invites root and crown rot (Phytophthora), so never plant in a low spot where water pools and lift the tree onto a low mound if your soil is heavy clay. Being deciduous, bare-rooted trees are sold and planted while dormant in winter (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water deeply and regularly once growth starts, and treat the stretch from flowering through fruit fill as critical, because a thirsty tree drops fruit and ripens it small. Feed a balanced complete fertiliser and go easy on nitrogen: too much drives soft leafy growth at the expense of fruit, and a heavy nitrogen feed close to harvest can delay ripening and dull the colour, so concentrate the feeding between petal fall and about a month before picking, then stop. Keep the soil slightly acid (about pH 6.0 to 6.5) and mulch well to hold moisture, keeping the mulch back from the trunk.

sections.heading: Pruning
sections.body: Train a young tree to an open vase or a central leader for light and air. The key cherry rule is when, not how: prune in summer or straight after harvest in dry weather, never in the cold wet of winter. Silver leaf fungus and bacterial canker, which can kill cherry limbs, release their spores in damp autumn and winter weather and enter through fresh cuts, so summer wounds carry far less risk and heal faster. Keep cuts small, work on a dry day, and dress any large wound.

sections.heading: Rain cracking and birds
sections.body: Two things threaten the ripening crop. Rain in the last weeks before harvest is taken up through the skin and swells the fruit until it splits, and split cherries then rot; there is no cure once it rains, so the defences are preventive: choose later-ripening or more crack-tolerant varieties, grow under rain covers in wet districts, and some growers spray calcium from pit hardening to firm the skin. Birds are the other certainty: ripe cherries are irresistible and an unprotected tree can be stripped in a day, so netting is essential, not optional.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Cherries are non-climacteric, meaning they do not sweeten or ripen any further once picked, so leave them on the tree until they are fully coloured and taste sweet, then pick them with the stalk attached to keep them longer. The Australian season runs from about late October to February, and most home trees fruit from November to January. A grafted tree usually starts bearing in its third to fifth year, and genetic dwarfs can fruit a year or two sooner.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than a seedling: cherries do not come true from seed and a seedling can take many years to fruit, if it ever fruits well. The rootstock sets the size, from the vigorous Mazzard and the semi-vigorous Colt (the usual Australian stock), which make trees of four to six metres, to dwarfing stocks such as Gisela that hold a tree to half that and crop a year or two sooner. Check the label for the variety, its chill needs, and whether it is self-fertile or needs a pollinator. Bare-rooted trees in winter are the cheapest way in, and a self-fertile variety or a multi-graft tree solves pollination in a small garden. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: How long until a cherry tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted, named cherry usually starts bearing in its third to fifth year and builds to a full crop over the few years after that. Genetic-dwarf types in pots can carry a little fruit a year or two sooner. A tree grown from a cherry stone is slow and unpredictable, which is why a grafted tree is worth the extra cost.

faqs.q: Can I grow a cherry tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, if you choose a dwarf. Genetic-dwarf cherries and trees on dwarfing rootstock stay small enough for a large pot on a sunny balcony or courtyard, and a pot makes it easy to net the tree and shift it out of harvest rain. Water and feed regularly, since pots dry out and lose nutrients fast, and remember a variety that is not self-fertile will still need a compatible partner nearby.

faqs.q: Why is my cherry tree covered in blossom but setting no fruit?
faqs.a: Most often it is a pollination gap or the weather at flowering. Many cherries need a second, compatible tree in bloom nearby, and cold, wet or windy days keep bees from moving the pollen. A spring frost on the open flowers will also cut the set. A warm, still bloom and a self-fertile variety give you the surest crop.

faqs.q: Will a late frost ruin my cherry crop?
faqs.a: It can. Cherries flower in early spring, and a hard frost on the open blossom or the tiny new fruit will cut the crop. In frost-prone gardens, plant on a slope or rise where cold air drains away rather than in a frost hollow, and be ready to throw a cover over a small or potted tree on a clear, still night when frost threatens.

WA.intro: Growing cherries in Western Australia. Cherries are the hardest of the stone fruits to grow in WA, because almost nowhere in the state gets the long, cold winter they need, but the coolest southern hill and forest districts do grow them, and quarantine rules shape where you can buy a tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Most of WA, including the warm Swan coastal plain, is simply too mild in winter to chill a traditional sweet cherry into a good crop, and warm, sometimes wet springs add to the difficulty. The cooler hills and the far south-west get materially more winter chill than the plain, and that is where cherries have a chance; even then, low-chill varieties are the safest choice for a backyard.

sections.heading: Where cherries grow in WA
sections.body: The main commercial cherries come from the Southern Forests around Manjimup and Pemberton, the coolest, highest-rainfall corner of the south-west, with smaller plantings in the Perth Hills around Pickering Brook, Bickley and Carmel, where the ranges lift the winter chill above the coastal plain. The Great Southern around Mount Barker is cool pome and stone fruit country too, though cherries there are a minor sideline.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA cherries ripen for the Christmas table, from about November into December and sometimes early January in cooler seasons. Late spring rain can split the early-ripening fruit, so growers watch the forecast closely as picking nears.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: The fly to plan around in WA is the Mediterranean fruit fly (Medfly), which is established right across the state and attacks ripening cherries, so most growers net or bait. WA has no established Queensland fruit fly and eradicates any incursion, so here, unlike the eastern states, it is Medfly rather than Queensland fruit fly that you manage. Watch also for pear and cherry slug skeletonising the leaves and bacterial canker on the limbs.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from interstate must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually certified and treated before they can enter, and cherry (a Prunus stone fruit) nursery stock is tightly controlled to keep diseases such as little cherry disease out of the state. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post cherry trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.q: Which cherry varieties have the best chance in Perth?
faqs.a: On the warm coastal plain your best bet is a low-chill variety such as Minnie Royal grown with its partner Royal Lee, since traditional high-chill cherries rarely get the winter cold they need near the coast. Up in the cooler Perth Hills you can also try self-fertile types like Stella or Lapins. Whatever you pick, a cool, airy site and netting against Medfly and birds matter as much as the variety.

faqs.q: Are cherries realistic for an average WA backyard?
faqs.a: Be honest about your winter. On the Swan coastal plain cherries are a gamble and a low-chill variety is the only sensible try; in the hills or the far south-west the odds improve a lot. If your heart is set on home-grown stone fruit and your winters are mild, a peach or a plum will crop far more reliably than a cherry.

QLD.intro: Growing cherries in Queensland. For almost all of Queensland the winters are far too warm for cherries, so they are a crop for one cool corner of the state: the Granite Belt.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Coastal, subtropical and tropical Queensland do not get anywhere near the winter chill a sweet cherry needs, so a tree may grow but it will flower thinly and set little or no fruit. The one real exception is the high, frosty country of the inland south, which gets genuine winter cold. Even the low-chill varieties want a cooler site than most of the state can offer.

sections.heading: Where cherries grow in QLD
sections.body: Cherries in Queensland come almost entirely from the Granite Belt around Stanthorpe and Applethorpe, high on the New England Tableland at around 800 metres, where winters are cold and frosty and growers also produce apples and other stone fruit. Pockets around Glen Aplin and The Summit grow the bulk of the state's small cherry crop.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: The Granite Belt cherry season is short and centres on December, running from late November into the weeks before Christmas. A wet spell as the fruit colours can split and rot it, so the crop varies a lot from year to year.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to the state and a serious pest of cherries, so netting, baiting and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit are essential. The Granite Belt's summer storms also bring rain cracking and brown rot in the ripening fruit, and birds need netting just as they do everywhere else cherries are grown.

faqs.q: Will cherries fruit in Brisbane or on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Not with any real success. Brisbane and the coast stay far too warm in winter to chill a cherry into fruiting, so even a healthy-looking tree will flower thinly and set almost nothing. If you want home-grown stone fruit in a warm part of Queensland, a low-chill peach is a far better use of the space.

faqs.q: Which cherries suit the Granite Belt?
faqs.a: The high country has enough winter chill for traditional sweet cherries, so growers there can plant the well-known high-chill varieties as well as self-fertile types like Stella and Lapins. Choose a compatible pair (or one self-fertile tree) for pollination, and lean to later-ripening kinds to dodge the summer storms that split the fruit.

NSW.intro: Growing cherries in New South Wales. NSW is one of the homes of the Australian cherry, but only in its cool tablelands: the warm coast and the hot western plains are too mild for a good crop.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Cherries want cold winters and a dryish run-up to harvest, which is why they thrive on the cool Central and Southern Tablelands and fail on the humid coast and the hot inland plains. Altitude is the key: the higher, colder districts deliver the winter chill and the firm, crack-resistant fruit that lower, warmer country cannot.

sections.heading: Where cherries grow in NSW
sections.body: Young, in the South West Slopes, is known as Australia's Cherry Capital and hosts the National Cherry Festival, and it has long been the centre of the state's crop. The other important districts are Orange and the surrounding Central Tablelands, the high, cold orchards around Batlow in the Snowy Valleys, and plantings near Mudgee and Bathurst.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The NSW season opens in the warmer districts in late October and early November and runs through to Christmas, with the coldest, highest orchards such as those at Batlow picking the latest, into the new year. The spread of altitudes gives the state a long cherry season overall.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is established through much of NSW and readily attacks cherries, so inland orchards run area-wide baiting and home growers net or bait. Pear and cherry slug skeletonises the leaves in summer, bacterial canker and brown rot show up in wet springs, and birds and harvest rain are the constant threats.

faqs.q: Which cherry varieties suit the NSW tablelands?
faqs.a: The cold tablelands suit the full range, so you can plant classic high-chill varieties like Bing alongside a self-fertile Stella or Lapins. Pick a compatible pair, or one self-fertile tree, for pollination, and in wetter spots lean to later, more crack-resistant kinds to ride out harvest storms.

faqs.q: Will cherries crop near the coast or in Sydney?
faqs.a: Not really. Sydney and the coast stay too mild in winter to chill a cherry properly, so trees there flower poorly and crop badly. Cherry growing in NSW belongs to the cold tablelands inland; close to the coast a low-chill stone fruit such as a peach will do far better.

VIC.intro: Growing cherries in Victoria. Victoria is the country's largest cherry producer, and its cold winters give the trees the deep chill they need, with the best fruit coming from the cool hills and the high country.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cold winters easily supply the chill that even high-chill cherries want, and the cool ripening season builds firm, well-coloured fruit. The main watch-outs are spring frost on the early blossom and rain at harvest in the wetter districts, while the warmest, lowest areas are more marginal for a dependable crop.

sections.heading: Where cherries grow in VIC
sections.body: Cherries grow right across the cooler half of the state. The Yarra Valley east of Melbourne, around Wandin, Silvan and Coldstream, is the best-known pick-your-own district; the cold north-east high country around Stanley and Beechworth grows prized late-season fruit; and there are orchards through the Goulburn Valley and central Victoria.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: The Victorian season runs from about mid-November in the Yarra Valley through to late January, with the cold high-country orchards near Beechworth picking the latest, often right up to Australia Day. The range of districts gives the state a long, staggered harvest.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is now established in parts of Victoria and is managed across the fruit-growing districts, and cherries are a known host. Pear and cherry slug, brown rot in wet weather, birds and harvest-time rain cracking round out the watch list for a Victorian grower.

faqs.q: Where can I pick my own cherries near Melbourne?
faqs.a: The Yarra Valley, less than an hour east of Melbourne, is the classic pick-your-own district, busiest around the Christmas and New Year holidays. For the latest fruit, the cool high country in the state's north-east keeps picking into late January. Orchards open for picking only while fruit is on the trees, so check before you drive out.

faqs.q: Which cherry varieties grow well in Victoria?
faqs.a: Victoria's reliable winter chill means you can grow almost any cherry, from classic high-chill varieties to self-fertile Stella, Lapins and Sunburst. Plant a compatible pair or a single self-fertile tree for pollination, and in the wetter hill districts choose later, more crack-tolerant kinds.

further_reading.title: The True Cherries: Description of Species (Martin Crawford)
